summary: Rava resolves the challenge from 13b: R. Yehuda's 'kol hayom' means the onen KG refrains from service the entire day as a rabbinic decree (lest he eat kodshim), distinct from Yom Kippur itself where no one eats. A side question about whether the provisionally-divorced backup wife is even an onen is resolved: even if not technically an onen, distraction alone disqualifies. The mishna then introduces the seven-day pre-Yom Kippur sequestration of the KG, during which he performs key Temple services daily. The Gemara asks who authored this mishna and rules it is not R. Akiva. The bulk of the page examines the paradox of para aduma (red heifer purification water): it purifies the impure but simultaneously renders the pure impure - how can the same substance have opposite effects?

display.title: Context determines the decree: 'kol hayom' restricts onen service only when eating is a real risk
display.oneLine: Rava resolves Abaye's challenge: 'kol hayom' bars the onen KG from service on a regular day (real eating risk) but not on Yom Kippur (the universal fast removes that risk).
display.whats: Rava resolves Abaye's challenge (from 13b): R. Yehuda's 'kol hayom' means the onen KG does NOT serve the whole day - a rabbinic decree to prevent him from accidentally eating kodshim. On Yom Kippur, when everyone fasts, there's no risk of him eating; but on a regular day when people eat, the onen might forget and eat. Therefore, on regular days the onen KG refrains all day. Rav Adda bar Ahava then raises a problem: R. Yehuda himself rules a backup wife should be prepared in case the original wife dies - yet if she dies on YK, the KG would serve as an onen without the eating-risk. Rava resolves: the two cases are different - YK's universal fast removes the eating risk.
display.hint: The decree 'lest he eat' only applies when there is a realistic temptation. On Yom Kippur, everyone fasts - the KG won't accidentally eat. On a regular day, when food is everywhere, a decree is necessary. Context determines the risk, and the decree tracks the risk.

learning.learnerQuestion: Why would a decree meant to prevent the onen KG from accidentally eating apply on a regular day but not on Yom Kippur itself?
learning.coreTension: R. Yehuda's backup-wife ruling implies the onen KG could serve on Yom Kippur without restriction, which seems to contradict Rava's all-day decree against onen service.
learning.coreMove: Rava distinguishes the contexts: the decree exists only where eating is a realistic temptation. On a regular day, surrounded by others eating, the onen KG might forget himself; on Yom Kippur, the universal fast removes that risk entirely, so the decree does not apply there.
learning.ahaMoment: Rabbinic decrees are context-sensitive: the same rule (don't serve as onen) can be necessary in one situation and unnecessary in another, based on the realistic risk involved.
learning.memoryAnchor: Everyone fasts on YK - even the onen won't eat. On a regular day, the aroma of food is everywhere, so the decree is needed. The fast eliminates the risk.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may read 'kol hayom' as one fixed rule. It is a context-sensitive decree whose scope tracks the actual risk of eating, not a constant restriction.

takeaway.text: A rabbinic decree against a feared outcome applies only where that outcome is a realistic risk. Remove the risk, here the universal Yom Kippur fast, and the decree no longer applies.

argumentFlow.label: Rava: 'kol hayom' = decree lest he eat (not a mitzva to serve)
argumentFlow.speaker: Rava
argumentFlow.text: 'Kol hayom' means the onen KG does not serve the whole day - a rabbinic decree to prevent him from eating kodshim. The decree is necessary on regular days (when eating is normal) but not on Yom Kippur (universal fast).

sourceRefs.note: Rava's revised reading of kol hayom

argumentFlow.label: Rav Adda: R. Yehuda's backup wife contradicts the decree
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Adda bar Ahava
argumentFlow.text: R. Yehuda says prepare a backup wife in case the original dies. If she dies on YK, the KG would serve as onen - and R. Yehuda doesn't issue a decree then. This contradicts the supposed decree!

argumentFlow.label: Context distinguishes: YK fast vs. regular eating
argumentFlow.speaker: Rava
argumentFlow.text: On Yom Kippur, everyone fasts - the onen KG will not come to eat. On regular days, everyone eats - he will come to eat. The decree follows the realistic risk.

argumentFlow.label: Does aninus even apply to the conditionally-divorced backup wife?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The backup wife was conditionally divorced - is she even considered an onen if her 'husband' dies? And even if not, distraction (itroda) alone would disqualify the KG from service.

concepts.def: A rabbinic decree lest he eat - the preventive measure that keeps the onen KG from serving on regular days, applied because of the realistic risk of accidentally eating kodshim when everyone else is eating.
Distraction, mental preoccupation - even without full onen status, extreme emotional distraction can disqualify the KG from Yom Kippur service because it affects his concentration and fitness.

requiresUnderstanding: R. Yehuda's backup-wife position and its connection to Yom Kippur

misconceptions.misconception: 'Kol hayom' means a mitzva for the onen KG to serve all day.
misconceptions.correction: Rava's revised reading is that it is a restriction, not a mandate: the onen KG refrains from service all day as a decree.

misconceptions.misconception: The distraction argument (itroda) requires formal onen status to apply.
misconceptions.correction: It applies independent of formal onen status, even if the backup wife's relationship to the KG is legally ambiguous.

visualizableElements.desc: Regular day (everyone eating, decree needed to prevent onen KG from eating) vs. Yom Kippur (universal fast, no risk, no decree needed)
The onen KG on a regular day vs. on Yom Kippur - same person, different context, different ruling

quizSeeds.question: According to Rava, what does R. Yehuda's phrase 'kol hayom' mean regarding the onen KG?
quizSeeds.answer: The onen KG refrains from service the whole day as a rabbinic decree lest he accidentally eat kodshim.

sourceRefs.note: Rava's revised reading of kol hayom

quizSeeds.question: Why does the 'lest he eat' decree not apply on Yom Kippur itself?
quizSeeds.answer: On Yom Kippur, everyone fasts - there is no food around and no realistic temptation for the onen KG to accidentally eat kodshim. The decree only applies when the general environment includes eating.

sugyot.finalRuling: On regular days: the onen KG does not serve as a rabbinic decree (lest he eat). On Yom Kippur: the decree does not apply (universal fast). Service performed is valid either way.

display.title: Seven days of practice: the KG's pre-Yom Kippur sequestration
display.oneLine: The mishna introduces the KG's seven-day Parhedrin sequestration, during which he performs the daily Temple services himself in preparation for Yom Kippur.
display.whats: The mishna: seven days before Yom Kippur the KG is sequestered in the Parhedrin chamber. During those seven days he performs the daily Temple services himself: throws the blood of the daily offerings, burns the incense, cleans the menorah lamps (meitev et ha-nerot), and offers the limbs. He also performs whatever other services he wishes throughout the rest of the year (he is given priority). The Gemara asks who authored this mishna.
display.hint: The seven-day sequestration serves two purposes: to protect against impurity (a seven-day impurity requires a clean seven days to pass) and to practice the Yom Kippur service. The KG performing the daily service throughout the week keeps him in ritual readiness.

learning.learnerQuestion: What does the KG actually do during his seven days of pre-Yom Kippur sequestration, and why does the Gemara immediately ask who authored this mishna?
learning.coreTension: The mishna's seven-day sequestration combines a purity safeguard with active daily service practice, a combination specific enough that the Gemara needs to identify which sage's view it follows.
learning.coreMove: The mishna lists the daily services the KG performs himself during the seven days (blood, incense, lamps, limbs) and grants him priority on services year-round; the Gemara then opens an inquiry into the mishna's authorship, answered by the next sugya's para aduma analysis.
learning.ahaMoment: The seven-day preparation is both legal (ensuring purity) and experiential (practicing the Yom Kippur avodah). The KG who has been doing the service all week enters Yom Kippur as a practiced professional.
learning.memoryAnchor: Seven days of service = seven days of practice + seven days of assured purity. The KG arrives at Yom Kippur as both pure and experienced.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may treat the seven days as passive isolation. The mishna specifies active daily service, making the week dual-purpose: purity assurance and practiced readiness for Yom Kippur.

takeaway.text: The KG's seven-day sequestration is active preparation, not passive isolation: daily service practice combined with the time needed to clear any contracted impurity.

argumentFlow.label: Mishna: KG's seven-day sequestration and daily service
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishna
argumentFlow.text: Seven days before Yom Kippur, the KG is sequestered in the Parhedrin chamber. He performs: throwing the daily blood, burning incense, cleaning the menorah (meitev nerot), and offering the limbs. He also offers whatever he wishes on other days of the year.

concepts.def: Seven days - the pre-Yom Kippur sequestration period, during which the KG is isolated in the Parhedrin chamber to ensure he arrives at Yom Kippur in a state of purity.
Cleaning the menorah lamps (removing old wicks and ash); one of the daily Temple services the KG performs during his seven-day preparation.

requiresUnderstanding: The seven-day impurity cycle that necessitates the sequestration
The daily Temple service and its components

misconceptions.misconception: The seven-day sequestration exists only for isolation from impurity.
misconceptions.correction: It also serves as active preparation through practice: the KG performs the daily Temple services himself throughout the week.

misconceptions.misconception: The KG merely observes the daily service during the seven days.
misconceptions.correction: He actively performs the daily services himself: blood, incense, lamps, and limbs.

visualizableElements.desc: Seven days of KG performing daily Temple services - building toward Yom Kippur service
The Parhedrin chamber in the Temple - the KG's sequestration quarters for seven days before Yom Kippur

quizSeeds.question: Name two Temple services the KG performs during his seven-day pre-Yom Kippur sequestration.
quizSeeds.answer: Any two of: throwing the daily blood, burning the incense, cleaning the menorah lamps (meitev nerot), offering the limbs of the daily sacrifice.

quizSeeds.question: Why is the pre-Yom Kippur sequestration specifically seven days?
quizSeeds.answer: To ensure that if the KG contracted impurity requiring a seven-day purification process, he would emerge purified before Yom Kippur.

sugyot.finalRuling: The KG is sequestered for seven days before Yom Kippur and performs the daily Temple services throughout that period.

display.title: Para aduma's paradox: one water, opposite effects, settles the mishna's authorship
display.oneLine: R. Akiva's purification-water paradox, which purifies the impure while impurifying the pure, conflicts with the seven-day mishna and rules out R. Akiva as its author.
display.whats: The Gemara deduces that the mishna about seven-day sequestration cannot follow R. Akiva. R. Akiva teaches that purification water (mei para) purifies the impure person sprinkled upon - but simultaneously renders the pure person handling it impure. If so, the KG cannot both be sprinkled with purification water (to ensure his purity) and serve in the Temple (since handling makes pure priests impure). Various opinions are surveyed: R. Yehoshua b. Levi says it is a chok (divine decree, no explanation); Rabba b. bar Chana says it purifies by decree. Ultimately the Gemara surveys who authored both this mishna and the later mishna about the Yom Kippur sprinkling, concluding they follow the view that accepts R. Akiva's paradox.
display.hint: The para aduma paradox is one of the Talmud's classic examples of a chok - a divine decree whose logic transcends human comprehension. It purifies the impure while impurifying the pure, seemingly contradicting itself. The Gemara uses this paradox to narrow down who could have authored various mishnayot.

learning.learnerQuestion: How can the same purification water both purify and impurify, and what does that paradox tell us about who authored the seven-day sequestration mishna?
learning.coreTension: If the KG is sprinkled with purification water while still pure, to guard against future impurity, R. Akiva's rule that such water impurifies the pure handler would make the KG impure and unable to serve, directly contradicting the mishna's premise.
learning.coreMove: R. Akiva derives from the doubled 'al' in Numbers 19 that purification water impurifies the pure as well as purifying the impure; the Sages read the same verse as a scope limitation only. Since the mishna's sequence requires the KG to be sprinkled and then serve, it must follow the Sages, not R. Akiva.
learning.ahaMoment: The same water does opposite things simultaneously - but to different categories of people (impure vs. pure). This is the quintessential chok: its mechanism is beyond normal cause-and-effect reasoning.
learning.memoryAnchor: Para aduma - the red heifer: washing the hands that wipe makes them dirty; here, the water that purifies the impure also dirties the pure. A divine decree that transcends logic.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners may try to rationalize the para aduma paradox into a consistent natural mechanism. The sugya treats it as a chok, an accepted divine decree whose opposite effects are simply received law, and uses it here only to settle authorship.

takeaway.text: Para aduma is the paradigm chok: the same water purifies the impure and impurifies the pure by divine decree, and this very paradox is used to determine that the seven-day sequestration mishna follows the Sages, not R. Akiva.

argumentFlow.label: Mishna authorship: cannot be R. Akiva
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The seven-day sequestration mishna (with purification water sprinklings) is incompatible with R. Akiva's teaching that purification water renders pure handlers impure. If the KG is sprinkled while still pure (to guard against future impurity), the water would make him impure.

argumentFlow.label: R. Akiva's paradox stated
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Akiva
argumentFlow.text: Purification water: 'al ha-tamei yitaher' (on the impure it purifies) but 'v'al ha-tahor yitamei' (on the pure it impu rifies). The water operates oppositely depending on who is contacted.

argumentFlow.label: Chakhamim: 'al ha-tamei' is a drasha, not a ruling
argumentFlow.speaker: Chakhamim
argumentFlow.text: The Sages say 'al ha-tamei yitaher' is a scriptural amplification teaching that purification water only works on things that can receive impurity - it doesn't imply the reverse effect on the pure.

argumentFlow.label: Baraita: who authored the Tamid mishna about remaining water?
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: A baraita about what to do with leftover water in the hyssop (if enough remains to re-sprinkle or not) aligns with R. Akiva's position, suggesting the detailed water-handling rules follow R. Akiva.

argumentFlow.label: Who impurifies whom: man on animal or animal on man?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: R. Akiva's ruling about the pure becoming impure: does sprinkling on a person make the animal impure, or does the water itself on a person have this effect? The distinction matters for what minimum quantity of water is required.

sourceRefs.note: Water on body vs. water in vessel

concepts.def: Purification water - the water mixed with red heifer ashes used to purify those who became impure through contact with a corpse. It both purifies (the impure) and impurifies (the pure handlers).
A divine decree without apparent logical explanation. Para aduma is the paradigmatic chok: its opposite effects (purifying/impurifying) transcend ordinary cause-and-effect.
'On the impure, it purifies' - the verse from Numbers 19 about purification water. R. Akiva derives from the extra 'al' that it also impurifies the pure; the Sages interpret it as a scope limitation.

requiresUnderstanding: The concept of tum'at met (corpse impurity) and its seven-day purification

misconceptions.misconception: Purification water simply purifies everything it touches.
misconceptions.correction: R. Akiva's teaching shows it has opposite effects depending on the recipient's status: it purifies the impure but impurifies the pure.

misconceptions.misconception: The paradox is a logical contradiction that needs resolving.
misconceptions.correction: It is a consistent rule: the water activates the appropriate transformation for whoever it contacts, impure becomes pure, pure becomes impure, accepted as a chok.

visualizableElements.desc: The same water: touching an impure person (purification occurs) vs. touching a pure handler (impurity occurs) - two directions, one substance
A kohen holding hyssop dipped in mei para - the water purifies the corpse-impure person being sprinkled but makes the sprinkling kohen impure
Numbers 19:19 'al ha-tamei yitaher' - the verse driving R. Akiva's paradoxical ruling

quizSeeds.question: What is R. Akiva's teaching about purification water that creates a problem for the seven-day sequestration mishna?
quizSeeds.answer: Purification water purifies the impure person sprinkled on it, but simultaneously makes pure people who handle it impure.

quizSeeds.question: Why is para aduma called a 'chok'?
quizSeeds.answer: Because its mechanism transcends logical explanation - the same substance produces opposite effects (purifying/impurifying) depending on the recipient's status, without an obvious natural cause.

sugyot.finalRuling: The seven-day sequestration mishna follows the view of the Chakhamim (not R. Akiva). R. Akiva's para aduma ruling creates an irresolvable conflict with the sprinkling-and-service sequence.

alternateAngles.angle: Para aduma as paradigm of chukim
alternateAngles.desc: This sugya is classically cited in discussions of chukim (supra-rational laws) - para aduma is the Talmud's go-to example of a divine decree that resists rational justification.

glossary.en: Acute mourner - from death of a close relative until burial. Forbidden from eating kodshim; the onen KG may serve on Yom Kippur (public duty overrides) but not eat sacred food.
Red heifer - the cow whose ashes, mixed with water, create mei para (purification water) for corpse-impurity. Paradigmatic chok: purifies the impure while making pure handlers impure.
A divine decree beyond rational explanation. Para aduma is the quintessential chok, cited here and throughout rabbinic literature as the limit of explanatory legal reasoning.
A rabbinic decree lest he eat - the reason the onen KG refrains from service all day on regular days; the risk of accidentally eating kodshim is realistic when everyone else is eating.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: continues 13b's truncated word - to say that he does not serve the whole day, until night. But at night, if he comes to burn
Rashi: continues - fats and innards, he may burn them. Then opens 'decree lest he eat' - all day it is forbidden to eat
Rashi: continues - consecrated foods by Torah law, but the mourning status of the night after the deceased was buried during the day is not
Rashi: continues - from Torah law but from the words of the Sages, and this is how we understand Rabbi Yehuda's view
Rashi: continues - in tractate Zevachim (99b), and this is what he says:
Rashi: continues - Rabbi Yehuda: the whole day he is in mourning status
Rashi: concludes - and he may not sacrifice. Then opens 'but she is divorced' -
Rashi: continues - as we established: that if he sees she is about to die, he precedes
Rashi: continues - and enters the synagogue, and makes
Rashi: continues - the get of the one who died retroactive. Then opens 'is he not' -
Rashi: continues - troubled? And for sacred offerings we require joy
Rashi: continues - and greatness, as it is written (Numbers 18), 'to you I have given them
Rashi: concludes - for anointing, in the way that kings eat
Rashi: concludes - (Chullin 132b). Then opens on the mishna's 'he sprinkles the' -
Rashi: continues - blood, of the daily offerings, so that he becomes accustomed
Rashi: concludes - and prepared for the Yom Kippur service. Then opens 'and he tends the' -
Rashi: continues - lamps: he removes the ash from the burnt wicks
Rashi: continues - that went out, and so every day he tends them in the morning,
Rashi: continues - as it says: 'morning by morning, when he tends
Rashi: continues - the lamps'; and every day they burn incense
Rashi: continues - in the Sanctuary, half in the morning and half in the
Rashi: concludes - afternoon. Then opens 'and on all the other days, if he wishes' -
Rashi: continues - to tend and to sacrifice any offering he wishes,
Rashi: continues - he sacrifices it, and the priestly watch cannot prevent
Rashi: concludes - him. Then opens 'he sacrifices a portion first' - 'first' means
Rashi: continues - to burn and to sacrifice any portion
Rashi: concludes - that he chooses. Then opens 'and he takes a portion' - in the distribution
Rashi: continues - of eating consecrated foods. Then opens 'first' - a choice portion
Rashi: concludes - that he chooses for himself. Then opens the Gemara section - regarding Rabbi Akiva's view,

Rashi: 'heichi avid avodah - how can he perform service (during the seven days)?' The problem: if the sprinklings happen during the seven days and make the pure KG impure (per R. Akiva), how can he serve?
Rashi: 'kol shivat hayamim mazin alav - all seven days they sprinkle on him.' The KG is sprinkled daily with purification water throughout his seven-day sequestration.
Rashi: 'al ha-tamei yitaher v'al ha-tahor yitamei - on the impure it purifies, on the pure it impurifies.' R. Akiva's paradoxical ruling: the same water operates oppositely on impure and pure people.
Rashi: 'k'detanan niskavein - as we learned: if one intended...' The baraita from Parah: if there is enough water left on the hyssop, one may re-use it without re-dipping.
Rashi: 'im yesh ba-ezov - if there is enough in the hyssop.' The minimum to count as a valid sprinkling is enough water to actually produce droplets that reach the recipient.
Rashi: 'yishneh - he may re-use it.' If enough water remains on the hyssop tips, the person who sprinkled may sprinkle again without re-dipping into the vessel.

Rashi: 'v'chakhamim omrim - and the Sages say: on the impure it brings about purification.' The Sages' reading of 'al ha-tamei yitaher': this phrase limits purification water to things capable of receiving impurity.

Rashi: 'l'omer she-ein divrei hazza'ah amurim ela b'devarim ha-mekabelim tuma'ah - meaning: the words of sprinkling apply only to things that can receive impurity.' The Sages derive a scope limitation, not R. Akiva's opposite-effect ruling.

Rashi: 'aval b'devarim she-ein mekabelim tuma'ah ein hazza'ah ha-nitozet aleihem keruya hazza'ah - but for things that cannot receive impurity, the droplets that land on them are not called hazza'ah.' A vessel that cannot receive impurity is not affected by sprinkling.

Rashi: 'v'nafka minah she-ha-shira'im she-ba-ezov shira'i pesulei hen - and the practical implication: leftover drops on the hyssop are invalid remnants.' They cannot be used for a second sprinkling because they contacted a non-receiver.
Rashi: 'v'ein l'hakhshiran l'hazzot - and there is no way to make them valid for sprinkling.' Once the drops have been used on an invalid surface, they cannot become valid purification drops again.

Rashi: 'k'detanan niskavein v'khulei im yesh ba-ezov yishneh - as we learned: if there is enough in the hyssop, he may re-sprinkle.' The baraita presupposes the drops can be re-used, aligning with R. Akiva's view.
Rashi: 'yishneh - he may re-sprinkle.' The baraita shows that drops remaining on the hyssop are still valid - this only works according to R. Akiva where they retain their purifying status.

Rashi: 'la yishneh - he should not re-sprinkle.' R. Akiva's ruling implies: if he sprinkled on an animal (which cannot receive impurity), those drops are now invalid - 'hanitztzah alav melakhah d'hazza'ah shel behemah ein shem hazza'ah alehah.'

Rashi: 'v'havah lehu k'mayim she-na'aseit bahen melakhah - they become like water in which a labor was performed.' Once used in a 'sprinkling' on an invalid target, they are disqualified as if used for a mundane purpose.

Rashi: 'l'mazeh tahor - for the one who sprinkles who is pure.' R. Akiva's ruling: even after sprinkling (which should make him impure), the verse still calls the sprinkler 'tahor.' Rashi explains the drasha.

Rashi: 'diderish hekhi v'hizzah ha-tahor af l'achar she-hizzah kara'o tahor - he derives: v'hizzah ha-tahor (Num 19:19): even after he sprinkled it calls him tahor.' The verse applies the 'tahor' label post-sprinkling.

Rashi: 'haniacha l'man d'amar... plugtah hi b'Zevachim b'perek ha-terovet - this follows one who says... it is a dispute in Zevachim chapter ha-terovet (80a).' The same question about whether post-action 'tahor' labels apply arises in another context.
Rashi: 'agba d'gavra - on the body of a person.' Any minimal amount of mei para is sufficient to purify when applied directly to a person's body, with no minimum volume required.
Rashi: 'kol she-hu she-noten alav metaharo - any minimal amount that lands on him purifies him.' The body contact rule: no minimum volume for person-contact purification.

Rashi: 'aval b'mana - but in a vessel.' When the purification water is in a vessel and the hyssop is dipped into it, a minimum volume is required - enough to dip the hyssop tips and sprinkle effectively.

Rashi: 'sheyitbil rishei giv'olin b'toch hamayim tivila she-tehei ba l'hatiz mimena - to dip the tops of the hyssop stems into the water with a proper dipping that would produce droplets for sprinkling.' Functional definition of the minimum.
Rashi: 'd'var mekubal hu - it is a received tradition.' The service order and the minimum water volume rules are transmitted as oral law, not merely derived by logical reasoning.
Rashi: 'plugtah hi b'Zevachim - it is a dispute in Zevachim.' The question of whether purification water on an animal counts as a 'work' that disqualifies it is addressed in Zevachim 80a.
Rashi: 'b'perek ha-terovet - in the chapter on mixed consecrated items.' The parallel passage in Zevachim analyzes when a post-action label ('tahor' or 'tamei') applies retroactively.
Rashi: further analysis of the vessel minimum - the functional definition (enough to dip and sprinkle) as the operative standard for purification water measurements.
Rashi: 'sheyitbil - so that he may dip.' Rashi restates the definition: the minimum is determined by functional sufficiency for the sprinkling action.
Rashi: continuation of the hyssop minimum discussion - confirming that body-contact requires no minimum while vessel-based sprinkling requires enough for the action.
Rashi: closing note on the para aduma section - these discussions set up the transition to the next subject (Abaye's resolution) on 14b.
